data.title: INCO: Why Avoiding This ETF Remains A Better Choice
data.description:
Columbia India Consumer ETF (INCO) is a passive ETF tracking an index called Indxx India Consumer Index. The index methodology is market weighted and does not really take anything else into account. This is what the index mentions as a methodology for its security selection.

The Indxx India Consumer Index is a maximum 30 stock free-float adjusted market capitalization weighted index designed to measure the market performance of companies in the consumer industry in India.

I would say that this index, due to its methodology, in substance largely has a utility for tracking the underlying sector of Indian equities, rather than anything else, especially for replicating the underlying portfolio. We can think of it as a tracking tool instead of considering it as a vehicle for allocation. The valuations of this sector still trade at a premium to broad-based Indian equities, and also considering recent developments in energy prices and currency depreciation, it would be better if investors avoid this ETF for the time being. An expense ratio of 0.75% for a passive index tracking fund looks on the higher side as well.

Moderate Performance

The index has significantly underperformed the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) over the last ten-year period. I don't think performance, when also taking into account the specific risk factors associated with investing outside the home country, would tend to justify these results. The reason for the underperformance also, to an extent, has to do with the depreciation of INR versus the dollar, a point which I would discuss further. And a ten-year period is not an entirely short horizon for a comparative performance evaluation of an ETF.

In addition, Indian equities have also not been performing well over more recent timeframes as well, both versus US equities and also on an absolute basis. The reason for this is the geopolitical trajectory, which led to a significant rise in energy prices, accelerating depreciation of the domestic currency, which was already under pressure. Foreign investors, at the same time, for the last few years have withdrawn significant liquidity from Indian markets, leading to subdued performances. Although that in itself does not reflect the fundamentals entirely, the fact is that current uncertainty inherent in the macro trajectory also implies caution, particularly for investors outside domestic markets, who also have to account for currency fluctuations. In addition, INCO is a sector-based Indian ETF which invests in the consumer sector, for which valuations are further elevated.

Portfolio and Valuations

The below-reflected top ten holdings of the fund constitute around ~50% of the overall portfolio. While sector-based allocation with the largest allocation to Automobiles does not suggest any demerit for the time being, still, however, when I take into account the top holding composition, there are a few aspects worth mentioning which also reflect the expensive valuations of underlying holdings. I don't know what exact figures the fund might have used in its fact sheet to suggest the P/E of ~16.58. That figure appears even below the MSCI India Index. That is not the case, however, at least with reference to the top ten holdings, which still constitute half of the portfolio. I think the overall P/E figure has to do with the ETF using harmonic weighting to calculate its earnings multiple. While that has some advantages, at the same time it reduces the influence of large values and can cause the headline number to seem smaller. To also reflect on that, we can see the P/B of INCO to be ~6x versus ~3x of the MSCI India Index, which already suggests expensive valuations. And when it comes to Indian markets, it is quite evident that consumers trade at premium valuations against broader equities. Therefore, the interpretation is simple: do not get confused by this seemingly lower P/E ratio from the fund’s fact sheet.

In addition, the ~1000x P/E, which you might be seeing for a company called Eternal, is not the result of any short-term earnings hit or an accounting adjustment leading to the said number. The company has an operating profit margin of ~2%, having just very recently turned profitable. This is what a pure-play market cap-weighted stock selection does. Titan, another company, is trading at ~80 P/E. While the company has performed structurally across the cycle in terms of business developments, still a ~80x P/E does not leave any room for a new investment allocation. The company with the lowest multiple is ITC Ltd., which appears to be okay, but the company still gets over ~40% of revenue from tobacco, a sector which gets impacted by many policy-related changes. This is not to remark on each company in the portfolio but to suggest that on an overall basis, the valuation of the underlying companies of the ETF, even after taking into account recent subdued performance, still appears to be at premium levels. The underlying portfolio composition also, therefore, even if we pause on the macroeconomic trajectory, still appears not to be worthy of an allocation on a standalone basis either. Even if India might warrant any allocation for any purposes, I don’t think it should altogether be done via this vehicle.

This is not to remark on each company of the portfolio but to suggest that on an overall basis the valuation of the underlying companies of the ETF, even after taking into account recent subdued performance, still appears to be high. The underlying portfolio composition also, therefore, even if we paused on economic trajectory, still appears not to be worthy of an allocation on a standalone basis either. Even if India might warrant any allocation for any purposes, I don’t think it should be done via this vehicle.

Challenging Macro Environment

Investors investing from the U.S., in addition to domestic influences and factors impacting the allocation, are further significantly disadvantaged due to constant depreciation of the domestic currency, the Indian Rupee ((INR), versus the USD, which even in times of decent performance reduces the net return for a U.S. investor. The issue is much more elevated when the domestic Indian equities underperform versus U.S. equities, which further enhances the degree of underperformance. Over the last twenty years, it is approximately a ~3.83% CAGR depreciation of INR versus USD.

Impact of Rising Energy Prices

Any rise in energy prices leads to a direct negative impact on both Indian equities and currency. Since India is an importer of oil and gas, any increase in energy prices directly leads to more capital outflows, which increases the current account deficit of the country. In addition, more dollars are required for the purchasing of energy, which further puts pressure on the Indian INR. This has a dual negative impact for U.S.-based investors. Moreover, on a structural basis, widening of the import bill and current account deficit also puts pressure on the fiscal front, which tends to impact infrastructure and other related developments of the country. Either the government might have to cut spending to keep the fiscal deficit within the targeted range or allow it to widen, which again is not a permanent solution. This relationship doesn't always stand to be linear, but broadly this still remains negative, even if the extent and magnitude don't always remain identical. The below graph suggests the impact of energy price hikes to be relatively quite high on India's GDP and inflation as well. Since India is a very large consumer of energy along with being a net importer, the underlying economic structure causes these impacts and is largely contingent in nature.

This report further highlights the impact on the domestic economy with the persistent elevation of Oil prices. At the time of writing this article, Brent is at ~$110 Bbl. The same was trading at around ~$60 level at the start of the calendar year.

The impact of currency, which I highlighted earlier, is further visible for the YTD so far for 2026. The INR is down by ~4.5% with reference to the U.S. dollar. This has resulted in INCO being down by around ~8% for a U.S.-based investor. While standalone performance wasn't good either, the currency drag further enhances the underperformance. Taking all of it into account, I do not think there remains any case to allocate to INCO for the time being. The portfolio allocation already looks elevated in terms of valuations. The broader uncertainty prevails, which also reduces the allocation case to India at present. The energy shocks, along with the consistent drag caused by INR depreciation, further decrease any relative appeal for Indian equities for the time being.

Possible Upside Due to Near-Term Swing

There are a few aspects which can still result in a sharp upmove in Indian equities. If energy prices moderate or geopolitical uncertainty is reduced, Indian equities can see a sharp rebound. This rebound would be largely based on macro variables and therefore would positively tend to impact most sectors and companies, without taking into account their underlying fundamentals, whether good or bad. In addition, since foreign investors have pulled out a lot of capital from India so far, any change in stance could lead to an influx of liquidity in the markets, which can also enhance the upside. Indian equities have a fair chance of seeing a rebound, which can disappoint investors exiting at these levels. On a structural basis, however, I still do not see any case for a market capitalization-weighted consumer index as a vehicle for allocating to India. A broad-based Indian ETF, such as the one tracking India's flagship index Nifty 50, might still be a relatively better proposition versus a sector-based index fund for allocating to India from outside.

In Conclusion

INCO is rated as a hold for now because it doesn't offer any compelling proposition for the time being, while macro aspects further reduce the appeal. The reason for not recommending this as a sell is largely due to a recent subdued performance in Indian equities, which leaves scope for a near-term upside. Leaving that aside, a sell rating would not have been inappropriate. An investor, therefore, if they are willing to exit at a point which on a near-term basis can in hindsight might look somewhat ill-timed due to a possible upside, may still want to switch the allocation and invest in something better. The Seeking Alpha Quant rating grades it as a Sell, which I do not have anything to disagree with.

data.title: Oracle: Investors Fear CapEx, But They Might Be Missing The Upside
data.description:
In December, I reiterated my strong buy rating for Oracle (ORCL). It is the second time I marked the stock a strong buy, and for the second time, the stock also declined following my strong buy rating. This time it dipped by 6.3% whereas the S&P 500 gained 5.1%. It brings the total decline in the stock price since I marked Oracle shares a strong buy to nearly 43%. So this is definitely not my best rating, and that is an understatement. With this type of underperformance and the price development diverging strongly from the rating, it is of paramount importance to revisit the valuation for Oracle.

Oracle Scales Business While Preserving Operating Margins

In the third quarter, revenues grew 22% to $17.2 billion, beating analyst estimates by $281 million. Cloud sales grew 44% to $8.9 billion, followed by 36% growth in software sales to $6.1 billion, while hardware and services sales grew 4% and 8% to $714 million and $1.4 billion. AI infrastructure revenues grew 243% and margins of 32% gross margins. We note that AI infrastructure has lower gross margins, meaning that Oracle’s gross margins are likely going to settle at a lower level. Operating expenses grew 20% to $11.7 billion, driven by a 66% surge in cloud and software costs. Operating income, however, grew 25% to $5.5 billion, showing that cloud growth did not result in operating margin compression as operating margins expanded one point to 32%. Earnings per share of $1.69 on a normalized basis exceeded analyst expectations by $0.10.

In some way, the quarterly earnings are not of major interest unless there is an extremely big beat or miss. What investors are mostly focused on is the capital intensity and how Oracle intends to finance the capital expenditures. The company raised $30 billion through bonds and mandatory convertible preferred stock as part of a wider $50 billion financing program. Furthermore, the company is working with a new contract model for which $29 billion in deals is already signed. This model is basically a way for Oracle to get new customers without blowing up their balance sheet.

They have three ways to do this:

  1. Customers bring their own hardware, with Oracle providing the data center, power, networking and the software stack.
  2. Upfront customer payments
  3. Partner-funded infrastructure

The company’s remaining performance obligation was $533 billion, driven by large-scale AI infrastructure projects.

Oracle’s Response To Saaspocalypse Is Weakly Supported

The company also pointed out that the Saaspocalypse is not going to affect Oracle because it would mostly affect SaaS companies that do not adopt AI.

In the context of that, I'll say a few words about the reported SaaSpocalypse. You've all heard the thesis or theory that new companies coding quickly using AI will spell the death of SaaS. I don't agree with that at all. I do think that AI tools and their coding capabilities would be a threat if we weren't adopting them, but we are and very rapidly. Oracle is using the best AI coding tools and the best developers not only to accelerate our SaaS business but also to deliver solutions that enable entire ecosystems across numerous industries. The use of AI coding tools inside Oracle is enabling smaller engineering teams to deliver more complete solutions to our customers more quickly. We are building brand-new SaaS products using AI and also embedding AI agents right into our existing applications suites.

I believe that this argument is weak at best. I am not saying that Oracle is heavily impacted, but just that the argument is not a great one. The real SaaS disruption could come from lower cost of building software, AI potentially reducing switching costs and value shifting from software to workflows, data and agents. Whether Oracle does or does not use AI does not invalidate the potential AI disruption. So there is pricing pressure risk, and every company on paper has the advantage of deploying smaller teams and developing faster. That does not provide Oracle with an edge in any way. A better defense would likely have been to simply point out mission-critical structured data, regulatory and workflow complexity, and the full stack solution that could provide Oracle with an integration advantage. So Oracle’s argument in my view is directionally correct but weakly supported.

Oracle’s Capital Needs Are Partially De-Risked

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Oracle, margins should substantially increase this year and next year but with a dip in the margins for 2028 driven by a disproportionate amount of AI infrastructure coming online and ramping up. The company’s main issue is the balloon CapEx. The company plans to raise up to $20 billion from selling shares which I have modelled in for 2027. Besides that, I have assumed that debt maturities through 2028 will be refinanced with roughly $17 billion in additional borrowings. That should provide the company enough funds to cover its capital expenditures. Shareholders have been concerned about this CapEx-heavy phase. However, we note that the net debt to EBITDA will actually fall in 2027 onward unless Oracle further accelerates CapEx. So in some way the rising debt is a bit of a false concern because the leverage has been worse before. If investors did not mind it in 2023-2024, then it also is somewhat less of an issue for the years ahead.

For Oracle, I can understand that some investors may not feel comfortable investing. However, if you value the stock according to its median EV/EBITDA over the past 3 years, then there should be around 33% upside with a $250.22 price target. Sales are expected to grow by over 30% annually, and the same holds for EBITDA. Free cash flow is going to be negative due to the high CapEx. That is perhaps something that also requires investor attention. Currently the negative free cash flow may look concerning on the company’s ability to repay debt. However, the CapEx is flexible. So if the market softens for some reason, then Oracle can, with some lag, adjust the CapEx lever. What you are left with in that case is tens of billions of dollars in free cash flow.

I previously had a strong buy rating. I am adjusting that now to a buy rating not solely due to the high capital needs and capital raise, but also because I have implemented tweaks to my scoring model that simply result in a buy rating rather than a strong buy rating.

Conclusion: Transformational Growth At A Cost

Oracle is going through a huge CapEx cycle, and that requires debt to be added beyond the levels seen by Q3 and potentially capital raises from shareholders. At the same time, we note that the CapEx lever is flexible in times when demand may taper, and what we see at Oracle now is a CapEx-heavy cycle to realize additional cash flow in the tens of billions of dollars. This may not be the most comfortable investment for everyone, but I believe the stock is a buy given that its leverage is not going to be substantially higher by 2028 compared to 2023-2024. In other words, EBITDA is growing meaningfully as well.

data.title: ERH: Utility Exposure Offers Growth Potential For This Income-Focused CEF
data.description:
The Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund (ERH) is a closed-end fund that tries to offer an attractive investment proposition for those individuals who seek to earn a high level of current income from the assets that are in their portfolios. The fund does fairly well at providing its investors with a respectable level of current income, as it boasts an 8.42% yield at the current share price. Obviously, this is substantially higher than the yield of nearly any index fund, and it is also higher than the yields of most common equities. In addition, this fund includes some inbuilt protection against inflation that a leveraged bond fund, which is another asset class that income-seekers may consider, is unlikely to possess. Finally, the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund may be able to benefit from the data center construction boom that is currently occurring across the United States. As such, it may have a lot to offer an investor today.

About The Allspring Utilities And High Income Fund

The website for the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund offers the following description of the fund’s strategy:

The fund allocates approximately 70% of its total assets to a sleeve that places a focus on common, preferred, and convertible preferred stocks of utility companies. Approximately 30% of the fund’s total assets are allocated to a sleeve of U.S.-dollar-denominated below-investment-grade (high yield) debt.

This description is clearly that of a hybrid fund that invests in utility companies as well as junk bonds. The description explicitly states that the fund’s utility exposure will be substantially larger than its junk bond exposure, and it also provides for the possibility that the utility second exposure will include both common equities and fixed-income securities, so long as the securities are issued by utility companies. However, in practice, we can expect the fund’s utility exposure to consist mostly of common equities. The fund’s most recent semi-annual report provides the following asset allocation for the fund as of February 28, 2026:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

0.09%

Common Stocks

88.45%

Corporate Bonds and Notes

26.43%

Loans

1.46%

Yankee Corporate Bonds and Notes

5.62%

Money Market Funds

2.66%

While the fund does have some debt securities issued by utility companies in its portfolio, we can see that it has far more exposure to common stocks than to any form of debt or fixed-income security. Furthermore, nearly all of the common stocks that were in the fund’s portfolio on February 28, 2026, were issued by utility companies. We can see that in the sector breakdown provided in the semi-annual report:

Common Equities by Sector

% of Net Asset

Energy

0.18%

Health Care

0.07%

Utilities

88.20%

Clearly, we can see that the majority of the fund’s assets are invested in common equities issued by utility companies. This is actually rather nice to see in an income-focused fund, and as regular readers likely know, I strongly prefer common equities issued by utility companies and midstream energy companies over bonds issued by any company for income. This is in spite of the fact that utility companies typically have lower yields than most debt securities. For example, as of the time of writing, the Utilities Select Sector Index (XLU) yields 2.58%. This is lower than the current 4.34% yield on the ten-year U.S. Treasury note, and it is lower than the yield on any U.S. dollar-denominated bond. The specific utilities that comprise the largest positions in the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund also have lower yields than the ten-year U.S. Treasury note. The fund’s most recent fact sheet provides the following list of the largest positions in the fund as of March 31, 2026:

As expected, all of these are publicly traded American utility companies. Here are the yields on their common stocks:

Company Name

Current Yield

NextEra Energy (NEE)

3.25%

2.25%

3.35%

American Electric Power Company (AEP)

2.82%

0.55%

Atmos Energy (ATO)

2.16%

2.82%

Ameren Corporation (AEE)

2.70%

The highest-yielding of these companies is Duke Energy, but as we can see, even its 3.35% yield is lower than that of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note. As a general rule, all U.S. dollar-denominated bonds trade at yields that exceed those of U.S. Treasury securities with the same maturity date. This is mostly because the United States is perceived as having no default risk, whereas even the most financially-sound of companies could default on their debt obligations in the event of a crisis. Thus, at first glance, it appears that utility companies would be an inferior choice for income investors compared to bonds.

However, the advantage possessed by utilities comes not from their current yields, but from the fact that many utility companies raise their dividends on a regular basis. This is the case with many of the utilities that comprise the largest holdings in ERH’s portfolio. We can see this here:

Company Name

5-Year Dividend Growth Rate

NextEra Energy

10.11%

Southern Company

2.95%

Energy Corporation

5.70%

Duke Energy

2.00%

American Electric Power Company

5.48%

Vistra Corp.

10.30%

Atmos Energy

9.28%

Sempra

4.13%

Ameren Corporation

6.98%

Traditional bonds do not increase or grow their coupons over time. While there are some variable-rate bonds that do change their coupon payments with time, the changes that these securities experience are determined by interest rate movements rather than net income growth of the issuing company. Furthermore, dividend growth has no theoretical upper limit. As long as the company’s profits are increasing and the company’s cash flows support it, a company can raise its dividend without limit. This can result in investors who have held a stock for many years receiving a 100% or higher yield on the original principal that they used to purchase the stock. A variable-rate debt security will never have a 100% yield on the original principal unless a hyperinflation scenario is occurring and interest rates have risen to incredibly high levels. It is this dividend growth that makes dividend-paying common stocks superior to bonds for income investors. Basically, after an investor holds a company’s stock for a few years and receives a few dividend increases, it is very possible for the common stock to have a higher yield based on the investor’s original principal than the bond that the investor might have chosen to purchase instead. As utilities have relatively stable cash flows and most of them have a history of growing their dividends over time, these can be ideal stocks to consider for this purpose.

The fact that utilities grow their dividends over time gives them another advantage over bonds besides just having a higher yield-on-cost over a few years. This is that the dividend increases help to counteract the loss of purchasing power that accompanies inflation. Regular readers will likely recognize this as one of my biggest criticisms of long-term bonds. As the coupons simply pay a fixed amount of money based on the face value, the coupon payments actually decline in real terms as inflation compounds over the life of the bond. With a dividend growth stock, the increase in the dividend helps to offset this effect. Thus, common equities issued by companies that increase their dividends regularly do a better job of sustaining an investor’s income in real terms than any bonds do. Over the past few days, I published two articles to Seeking Alpha (see here and here) that explain why I expect the money supply to continue to increase and make inflation a permanent fixture of our lives going forward. As such, I believe that investors who want to earn income from their portfolios should stick to common equities that grow their dividends over time and generally eschew bonds. The fact that the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund has a greater exposure to utility stocks than to bonds is nice to see given this outlook.

Utility companies in general enjoy a great deal of protection against inflation. There are a few ways that they can do this, one of which is that many utility companies have fuel adjustment clauses built into their rate structure. This allows a utility to adjust the rate that it charges its customers to offset changes in the price of fuel or purchased power. Basically, whenever the cost of the fuel that a utility company uses to generate electricity increases or whenever the cost of electricity that it purchases from third parties (due to not having sufficient generation capacity) rises, the utility can pass those costs directly onto its customers. The utility is not allowed to make a profit doing this, but it does ensure that the utility does not have to sacrifice its margins or financial stability in the event of rising energy prices. As energy commodity prices usually increase during an inflation scenario, this provides the utility with protection against inflation. The second way in which utility companies are protected against inflation comes from the fact that they can request rate increases from regulators whenever costs rise due to inflation. Regulators have a vested interest in ensuring the financial stability of utility companies, and so they allow the utility to earn a specific rate of return. Whenever costs increase due to inflation, the company’s margins and rates of return decline if it is not allowed to increase rates. Regulators do not like this because it weakens the financial stability of the company, so they usually allow the utility to raise rates to offset the rising costs of wages and equipment in inflationary environments. However, this can be a slow process, so utilities might have to absorb the rising costs for a year or two before they are allowed to raise rates to compensate. While this prevents utilities from having perfect protection against inflation, it is still better than what companies in many other economic sectors possess.

As utilities offer superior inflation potential to bonds, and utilities have the potential to offer higher yield-on-cost after a few years than bonds, readers might conclude that this fund’s 33.60% of net assets exposure to debt securities is a weakness. That is probably true over the long term. After all, the common equity securities issued by utility companies offer both dividend growth and capital appreciation. Bonds offer a flat coupon and no net capital gains over their lifetimes (the bond is issued at face value and pays face value upon maturing). Thus, utility company equities should offer better total returns, and the bonds in ERH’s portfolio could be a drag on its lifetime total returns. Indeed, the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG), which invests almost exclusively in common equities of utility companies (UTG’s website states that it invests in both common equity and preferred equity, but it only has 0.80% of its net assets invested in preferred equity right now), outperformed ERH by a substantial margin over the past decade:

Thus, it does appear that the fund’s bond exposure could very well reduce its performance over the long term. However, there is one benefit that the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund derives from having exposure to junk bonds. This is that junk bonds have higher current yields than utility stocks. As already mentioned, the Utility Select Sector Index has a 2.58% yield. The Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK), which tracks the price and yield of an index of high-yield bonds issued by American companies, has a 6.58% yield at its current price, far above that of the utility sector. The fact that the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund has some of its portfolio invested in high-yield bonds therefore results in it having a higher level of income than would be the case if the fund were entirely invested in utility common equities. This supports a higher distribution yield. The Reaves Utility Income Fund only has a 5.69% yield compared to the 8.42% yield of ERH. This is the tradeoff. The Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund provides its investors with a greater level of income that they can spend today than pure-equity utility funds. However, investors will have to sacrifice total return to obtain this higher level of income. Whether this trade-off is acceptable or not depends a lot on your particular goals.

Utility Companies To Benefit From Data Center Construction

Over the past year or two, there have been a number of stories in the investment media regarding an impending surge in electricity consumption in both the United States and internationally. This has not escaped the notice of regulators and policymakers throughout the country, who have expressed concerns that the currently existing electrical grid will need to be upgraded to handle higher demand for energy, although the concerns of policymakers have generally revolved around two key points:

  1. Electric utilities will need to construct significant amounts of new electric generation capacity to support higher consumption, and these same utilities might have to upgrade their transmission and distribution infrastructure to carry the generated electricity to consumers.
  2. Electrical utilities are typically able to increase the rates that they charge their customers in order to recoup the money that they invest in generation, transmission, and distribution capacity.

The concern is that the impending growth in electricity consumption will cause residential customers to suffer from much higher power bills at a time when many households have already been ravaged by the inflation that has occurred over the past few years. LendEDU’s 2025 Personal Finance Survey found that 53% of Americans are living paycheck-to-paycheck, which means that all of the income is spent by the time their next regular payment arrives. Admittedly, this figure comes from a survey, so it is merely representative of what people are claiming about their personal financial situations. It is very possible that households are either weaker or stronger than this number would suggest. However, it does allow us to draw the conclusion that American households and consumers cannot really afford to pay higher electric bills, and this is what has gotten politicians and regulators so concerned. The surge in electric consumption is not coming from households, either, but from datacenters owned and operated by large corporations. If already struggling households have to pay higher electric bills due to the need to provide electricity to data centers owned by giant technology companies, then it risks becoming a political flashpoint. Regardless of your personal views on the matter, it may be worth paying attention to the actions taken by regulators in response to public opposition to rate increases, as it could impact the companies in your portfolio.

On April 14, 2026, PowerLines’ released a report analyzing the capital investment plans of 51 investor-owned utilities (i.e. the companies whose common stocks are owned by a fund such as ERH) and found that they are planning to spend at least $1.4 trillion over the 2026 to 2030 period on infrastructure upgrades and generation capacity to meet the power demands of the technology industry’s data center expansion and, in some areas, large industrial users of electricity. According to Charles Hua, Founder and Executive Director of PowerLines:

Investor-owned utilities are signaling a record-breaking wave of capital spending, and history shows that those plans are often a leading indicator of future utility rate increases.

This means that, as the utilities execute on their proposed capital spending plans, they will be requesting rate increases from regulators to recoup their expenses. If these rate increases are granted, then it will result in revenue and earnings growth for electric utilities. That could have a positive impact on the companies that are held in the portfolio of the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund. As stated earlier, the fund’s semi-annual report states that 88.20% of the fund’s net assets were invested in utilities as of February 28, 2026. The majority of these were electrical utilities, as shown here:

Utility Type

% of Net Assets

Electric Utilities

54.97%

Gas Utilities

4.23%

Independent Power and Renewable Electricity Producers

4.78%

Multi-Utilities

22.66%

The only pure-play natural gas utility in the fund’s portfolio as of February 28, 2026, was Atmos Energy. The rest of the utilities in the fund’s portfolio were either electric utilities (we can consider a renewable electricity generation company to be an electric utility) or multi-utilities. A multi-utility is a company, such as CMS Energy (CMS) or WEC Energy (WEC), that operates as both an electric and a natural gas utility, although certain parts of its service territory may only have the company operating as one of the two. A multi-utility should benefit from growing electricity consumption just like a pure-play electric utility will.

The expected growth in electricity consumption driven by data centers is potentially enormous. Last Friday, Business Insider published a report predicting that electricity consumption in Texas could quadruple by 2032. This is primarily driven by datacenters that are connected to the electrical grid, many of which have already requested hookups. The Midcontinent Independent System Operator, which manages the electrical grid for fifteen states in the Midwest, South, and Rocky Mountain regions of the United States, expects electricity consumption in its service territory to increase by 35% by 2035. As is the case in Texas, the electric demand increase in the Midcontinent Independent System Operator’s network will be driven by new datacenters being constructed to provide processing support for artificial intelligence applications.

As we all know, the amount of money that a person or company has to pay on their electric bill directly correlates with the amount of electricity that they consume. The more electricity that they consume, the more that they have to pay. Thus, we can see that the revenues of electric utilities should increase going forward as the planned data centers are constructed and connected to the electric grid. This is the reason for the $1.4 trillion worth of capital spending that the utility sector is expecting to make through 2030 – it is attempting to get the infrastructure necessary to service the higher expected electricity consumption. However, there are a few risks to this thesis.

One of the biggest risks may be regulatory and political. As already mentioned, electric utilities typically request rate increases in order to recoup their capital expenditures. Nationwide, the average residential electric bill has risen about 40% since 2021, and further rate increases could very easily result in public opposition to datacenter construction or the enactment of laws meant to shield residential customers from the rate increases that would be necessary for utilities to recoup expenses. Depending on exactly what these regulations say, it could result in this thesis playing out in a different way than outlined above.

For example, as was already stated, the Electric Reliability Council of Texas expects power demand in the state of Texas to quadruple by 2032. There are a few large electric utilities serving Texas:

  1. Entergy
  2. Oncor Electric Delivery (a private company that is majority owned by Sempra)
  3. Centerpoint Energy (CNP)
  4. American Electric Power
  5. TXNM Energy (TXNM)

It is very likely that one of these companies will be the primary electric provider to any datacenter that is constructed in Texas. If we refer back to the largest positions in the portfolio of the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund, we can see that the fund has positions in a few of these names. However, if the Texas regulators were to pass a law or impose some sort of restriction that datacenter owners do not like, it is possible that they would opt to construct their facilities elsewhere, which would, of course, prevent the Texas utility company from benefiting from the electric consumption growth.

Around the middle of last year, Texas passed a law that requires any data centers in Texas to accept curtailments should a load shed event occur. As Entergy explains on its website:

When the demand for electricity nears available supply levels, it is sometimes necessary to temporarily interrupt the delivery of electricity to maintain the integrity of the electric grid and to prevent catastrophic grid failures and extended outages for customers. This is called “shedding load.”

The Texas law requires any facility that requires 75 megawatts or more of electricity to power down or switch to backup generation (which will probably be located on site) if a utility requests it to do so. The reduction in the consumption caused by a datacenter switching to backup generation frees up electricity for residential or small commercial customers that are less likely to have backup generation (or are less able to pay for the fuel needed to power their backup generation). While taking a data center completely offline would be likely to adversely affect the technology company’s customers, most data centers are constructed with backup generation included simply to keep the computer equipment running in the event of a power outage. This allows the system administrators to power down the equipment safely (or keep it running through the power outage if the backup generators are powerful enough). Admittedly, I doubt that this particular law will have much of an impact on technology companies’ planning process regarding the location where they place their datacenters. However, it is not difficult to imagine a law that could cause them to change their minds about putting a data center at a specific location.

Another risk to our thesis that the aggressive datacenter build-out will benefit the nation’s electrical utilities is if more companies follow the technique that Alphabet (GOOGL) decided to use for one of its datacenters. About a week ago, the Energy Network Media Group published an article stating that it has decided to simply put a natural gas-fired electric generation plant at the site of one of its new data center campuses. According to the article:

Crusoe has already filed for a permit to develop the 933-MW plant on the site of the Goodnight campus, which will allow the facility to operate off-grid and is expected to provide energy for at least two buildings.

If a datacenter has its own on-site power generation, it may not need an electric utility to supply it with power at all. If enough datacenter owners start to adopt similar strategies, it will result in lower growth for the electric utilities than would otherwise be the case. However, with that said, utilities and interconnection operators tend to engage in extensive discussions with large electric users prior to the connection of a large datacenter, a new factory, or a similar facility. As such, they can usually formulate pretty accurate forecasts of future electric demand. We can therefore be relatively certain that the utility sector’s current plans of investing approximately $1.4 trillion through 2030 in infrastructure upgrades and new generation will produce a positive return on investment and continued growth for the companies involved. This should also support utility dividend growth, which will help grow ERH’s portfolio income over time.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund was on November 24, 2025. The fund did fairly well through late February, but then the Iran War began and caused most assets in the market to sell off. This fund was among them, as its share price declined 5.98% from February 27, 2026, through March 31, 2026. It rebounded in April, and is actually up 0.69% since the last time that we looked at it:

Unfortunately, though, we can see that this fund underperformed the S&P 500 Index as well as the Utilities Select Sector Index, at least in terms of price performance.

We do see an improvement if we include the fund’s distributions in the performance calculation, but it did still do worse than both indices:

Overall, we see that investors who opted to reinvest all of the distributions that the fund paid out over the approximately five-month period have seen their wealth increase by 4.28%. That is not a bad return for five months, but it is still a bit worse than would have been achieved by investing in a large-capitalization utility index fund.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. Unlike many funds with an income objective, however, the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund does not pay a stable monthly distribution. Rather, this fund pays a variable distribution of whatever amount would give it an annualized yield of 8% based on its net asset value over the past twelve months. This tends to result in the fund’s distribution growing during periods of time in which the share prices of American utility companies are rising. For example, this chart shows the fund’s distribution for each month since the end of 2024:

As we can see, the fund’s distribution has generally been growing over the period, and most months saw the fund pay a slightly larger distribution than the month prior. If utility companies do end up benefiting from datacenter-driven growth, we can expect that the fund’s distribution will continue to increase going forward.

As the fund distributes an amount of money that is sufficient to give it an annualized 8% yield, it needs to generate a total annual return of 8% or higher on average in order to sustain itself over the long-term. That is lower than the long-term average for the S&P 500 Index, so this should be possible. However, we should still look at the fund’s financial reports in order to determine how well it is actually doing at covering its distribution. We do not want it to be earning a lower return than it needs and destroying its net asset value in the process, after all. As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report for the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2026. This is a newer report than the one that was available the last time that we looked at this fund, so let us have a look at it and see how well this fund is doing at covering its distribution.

For the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2026, the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund reported a net investment income of $1,647,218. That was not sufficient to cover the $4,427,474 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

The fund was able to somewhat make up for the difference through capital gains, fortunately. For the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2026, ERH reported net realized gains of $1,904,323 along with an additional $10,147,299 in net unrealized capital gains. The fund had total investment profits of $13,698,840 over the six-month period:

While this is enough to cover the distributions, we should be cautious, as the fund was only able to cover the distributions because of its net unrealized gains. Over the six-month period, this fund only had net investment income and net realized gains of $3,551,541. That was not sufficient to cover the distribution. The fund actually ended up paying out all of its net investment income, net realized capital gains, and $875,933 of its unrealized gains. As unrealized gains can be erased by market weakness, there is the possibility that the unrealized gains that the fund is relying on to cover its recent distributions will be lost before it has a chance to realize them. However, given that the unrealized gains during the period vastly exceeded the amount of money that the fund needs to cover the distribution, it is probably fine. This fund should not have any real difficulty maintaining its distributions.

Valuation

Shares of the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund are currently trading at a 1.02% premium over the last reported net asset value. This is, obviously, a substantially higher price than the 9.50% discount on net asset value that this fund has had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is also fairly expensive compared to its long-term average valuation. Over the trailing five-year period, shares of the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund traded at prices ranging from a 16.82% discount to a 17.59% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 5.68% discount on net asset value, which is a better price than the fund has today.

Here is how ERH’s current price compares to the price of a few other utility-focused closed-end funds:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

1.02%

Reaves Utility Income Fund

0.26%

Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund (DPG)

0.34%

Gabelli Utility Trust (GUT)

0.81%

BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI)

0.18%

As of the time of writing, all of these funds are trading at slight premiums to their last-reported net asset values. This means that any investor who purchases one of these funds at the current price will actually pay more than the fund’s underlying assets are actually worth. That is a rather steep price to pay, and as such, it may make sense to wait for a little while for the shares to start trading at a discount on net asset value before buying.

Expenses

ERH’s semi-annual report states that the fund has an annualized expense ratio of 2.14%. As is the case with most closed-end funds, this one employs leverage as part of its investment strategy and needs to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed. If we exclude the interest expenses, then the fund’s expense ratio was only 0.92% annualized in the most recently reported six-month period.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund is an interesting closed-end fund that combines the common equities of utility companies with junk bonds. This strategy has the effect of providing the fund with a higher yield off of its portfolio than a pure utility fund would have, but it also seems to reduce its performance relative to such funds. The bulk of the fund’s portfolio is invested in utility common equities, which allows it to provide some protection against inflation that a bond fund lacks, and the fund has managed to steadily increase its distribution over the past few years. Overall, this fund could be a decent option for someone who would otherwise consider purchasing a bond fund, but it is best to wait for a discount before buying.

data.title: ING Groep: Higher Rates In Europe Can Provide Attractive Upside Potential
data.description:
As I’ve covered back in August 2024, I was not particularly bullish on ING Groep’s (ING) shares at the time due to some rate headwinds and, in my opinion, some better income alternatives in the European banking sector. Despite that, its shares have performed quite well, being up by more than 58% since then.

At the time I suggested Danske Bank (DNKEY) as a better income play within the European banking sector, as the Danish bank was selling its Norwegian operations and was going to return excess capital to shareholders. This recommendation has played out relatively well, considering that Danske is up by more than 65% during the same period and has outperformed ING slightly, even though there is a strong correlation between the share prices of the two banks, as can be seen in the next graph.

This happens because both banks are quality plays within the European banking sector and are naturally exposed to rates from the European Central Bank (ECB). While Danske is based in Denmark, the country has a peg to the euro and follows more or less the same monetary policy as the ECB.

While interest rates were declining in Europe when I last covered ING, being a main reason why I was cautious on its investment case, this has changed in recent months as the ECB has maintained rates unchanged since June 2025, and, due to recent energy price pressures, the market is expecting some rate hikes from the ECB ahead.

Indeed, current forecasts are for two hikes over the next few months to a level of about 2.65% by year-end 2026, which can be a tailwind for banking net interest income (NII) over the coming quarters.

While ING is not among the European banks more sensitive to rates, a higher interest rate environment is positive for NII growth ahead, potentially being an important support for revenue and earnings growth.

Having said that, ING had a resilient performance during a period of rate headwinds, as total revenues reached a new record during 2025, being a record year for the third consecutive year. This shows that its strategy to diversify its business and push for fee growth has been the right one, making its revenue profile less sensitive to rates than it was in the past.

As can be seen from the previous graph, ING’s revenues increased considerably in 2023 when rates increased rapidly in Europe to offset inflation pressures, with revenues increasing 21% compared to 2022. However, its revenues were quite resilient last year during a declining interest rate environment, which is a very good outcome, rising by 1.8% YoY to €23 billion.

This is justified by the bank’s loan book profile, with most loans set at fixed rates with re-settings at three to five years, smoothing the impact of lower rates in the short term, plus the bank’s initiatives to hedge its rate sensitivity. Nevertheless, ING’s NII declined a little bit during the year to €15.3 billion, down by 1.3% YoY, as lower rates had a negative effect on the bank’s net interest margin.

However, considering that about 66% of revenues are generated by NII, ING showed a very good top-line performance during 2025, as the push to grow fees related to daily banking activities, investments, and insurance was more than enough to offset NII weakness.

Fee income was the main reason for ING’s revenue growth during 2025, considering that fee income increased by 15% YoY to €4.6 billion, with fees now representing more than 20% of total revenues. Other income sources were stable during the year, even though revenues from capital markets were strong due to relatively high levels of customer activity.

For 2026, ING’s guidance is somewhat conservative, as the bank is expecting annual revenues of about €24 billion, up by 4.3% YoY. This expectation was largely driven by continued fee income growth, as the bank is expecting 5-10% fee growth during 2026 and some NII growth supported by loan book expansion and higher liability margin from its hedges and lower deposit costs.

However, investors should note that ING’s guidance was presented at the end of last January, and the recent Iran war and the spike in energy prices have resulted in inflationary pressures and higher odds of rate hikes ahead. In my opinion, this means that ING can eventually deliver higher NII growth in 2026 than it expected some months ago if the ECB eventually increases rates over the next few months.

The market doesn’t seem to be pricing this scenario yet, given that current analysts’ estimates are for ING to deliver revenues of about €24.1 billion in 2026, practically in line with the bank’s guidance. As the bank is expected to report its Q1 2026 earnings over the coming days, I think it’s possible that ING may increase its revenue guidance for the full year if it assumes current rate forecasts into its earnings model, potentially being a cyclical tailwind for positive revenue and earnings revisions in the short term.

From a structural point of view, ING’s growth prospects are relatively limited, and the bank acknowledges that profile, aiming to grow earnings by operating more efficiently and reduce costs by using automation and AI across its operations. The bank has been investing considerably in technology over the past few years and is one of the European banks that embraced digital banking more rapidly, a trend that is not expected to change much in the future. ING is investing in agentic AI to improve customer service, for instance, which is leading to a reduced need for headcount to offer banking services and products to customers.

This explains why ING reduced its staff during 2025, with further reductions expected over the next few years, being the primary driver of cost savings of 3% of operating expenses during 2025. Despite that, its total costs increased by more than 4% YoY due to wage inflation and higher other costs, leading to a cost-to-income ratio of 54.6%. While this is an acceptable ratio, it’s higher than that of the most efficient banks in Europe, showing that ING still has some room to improve operating efficiency ahead. For 2026, ING expects to cut about 1,250 staff positions, which should positively impact its operating jaws and profitability.

Regarding credit quality, this has been quite stable over the past few years as both households and corporates have maintained sound financial health despite some macroeconomic headwinds, explaining why its cost of risk ratio has remained stable at 19 basis points (bps) in 2025, compared to 18 bps in the previous year. As the loan book has grown over the past few quarters, this has resulted in provisions for loan losses of €1.3 billion in 2025, an increase of 9.2% YoY. This had a slight negative impact on the bank’s bottom line, but credit quality is not expected to deteriorate much in the near term and therefore is not likely to impact much its earnings in 2026.

Taking into account this profile of higher revenue but increasing costs and loan loss provisions, ING’s net income was €6.3 billion in 2025, representing a decline of 1% YoY. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, was 13.2% in 2025, being an improvement of 20 bps compared to the previous year. For 2026, the street is currently expecting a net income of about €6.5 billion, which means ING should report low-single-digit growth, with some upside potential to current estimates potentially coming from higher NII over the next few quarters. ING is expecting a ROE ratio of around 14% in 2026, being a small improvement compared to 2025.

Considering this profitability level and relatively muted growth prospects in the near term, I see ING’s current valuation of 1.36x book value as appropriate considering its historical ROE around 13%. If the bank eventually improves its ROE to a level between 14-15%, I see some upside potential to a valuation range around 1.5-1.6x book value, implying upside potential of about 15-25% if ING can deliver higher profitability over the next couple of years.

Having said that, investors should note that ING has not traded above 1.4x book value over the last decade, as can be seen in the next graph. If ING cannot sustainably increase its ROE to higher levels in the near future, then its shares seem to be fairly valued right now, and a higher multiple may be difficult to justify.

Another reason for investors to consider ING’s shares is its relatively high dividend yield, as the bank has increased its dividend over the past few years, and despite a strong share price rally in the recent past, it still offers an attractive yield for shareholders.

Over the past year, ING has distributed a total dividend of €1.25 per share through three dividend distributions (interim, bonus, and final dividends). At its current share price, this leads to a dividend yield of about 5.3%, which is quite attractive to income-oriented investors.

This dividend is also supported by the bank’s good capital position, given that it had a CET1 ratio of 13.1% at the end of 2025, above its 13% target, and its payout ratio was less than 60% based on 2025 figures, being a conservative level. Therefore, ING’s dividend seems to be sustainable, but dividend growth is likely to come from higher earnings rather than a higher dividend payout over the next few years, as the bank’s strategy is to have a conservative payout ratio to maintain some degree of financial flexibility.

Conclusion

ING Groep N.V. is one of my favorite banks in Europe, and while I was cautious a couple of years ago due to some rate headwinds, the bank’s top-line performance has been more resilient than I was expecting as ING reported record revenue pushed by fee income over the past few quarters. As the interest rate cycle seems now to be again for higher rates in Europe over the coming months, ING seems now to be more attractive from a cyclical perspective. If the bank can deliver a higher ROE over the next couple of years, it can have interesting upside potential, plus it offers a high-dividend yield that seems to be sustainable over the long term, making it a good play in the European banking sector.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AllianceBernstein: Dividend Drops, But Units Still Decently Priced
data.description:
AllianceBernstein (AB) reported mixed Q1 results on Tuesday, April 28. The stock traded slightly lower in the premarket, about on par with price action in recent months. A somewhat startling headline was that its dividend declined by 13.5% to $0.83.

Back in November 2024, I had a "Buy" rating on the now $3.5 billion market-cap Financials-sector stock. With a high 10.1% current dividend yield, AB has returned 15% since my previous analysis, underperforming the S&P 500 by about 5 percentage points.

Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. I’m reducing my fair value target, however, given soft EPS growth trends lately and a weak chart. The margin of safety is narrower amid ongoing outflows from active strategies. Today, I’ll offer color on the just-released Q1 report, refresh my valuation, and provide an update on the technicals.

AB Lags The S&P 500 YoY, Performing In-Line With The Financials Sector

In April, AB reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.83 was in line with the Wall Street consensus estimate, while revenue of $871 million, up 4% from the same period a year earlier, was a $23.6 million miss. The net sales gain was primarily due to higher investment advisory base fees, lower investment losses, and higher unitholder servicing fees, partially offset by lower performance-based fees.

Units inched lower in the premarket session, and if that holds, it would mark a third consecutive small post-earnings dip. Coming into the Q1 print, the options market had priced in a small 3.6% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring this week. Implied volatility is also in check at 28%, while short interest is low at 0.3%

Looking back on the quarter that was, AB reported a decent start to the year, with Q1 GAAP net income rising to $0.92 per unit, a big 37% increase YoY. Despite a volatile geopolitical climate that led to firmwide net active outflows of $6.3 billion, largely driven by redemptions in growth-oriented U.S. equities, the company saw strong momentum in its Municipal franchise and Private Markets.

Big picture, the partnership’s total AUM reached $838.6 billion, supported by an institutional pipeline that hit a record high of over $27 billion. While market volatility pressured Active Equity, the firm's adjusted operating income grew 3% to $291 million, allowing for a cash distribution of $0.83 per unit to be paid on May 21 (down from the previous $0.96 dividend). CEO Seth Bernstein called out that the firm remains well-positioned through its diversified investment capabilities and expanding private wealth and insurance partnerships.

AB: Color On Quarter

A major strategic highlight was the announcement that AB will eventually manage $100 billion in incremental assets through the Equitable-Corebridge merger, which should buttress its flywheel for long-term growth and earnings durability.

What’s more, its Private Wealth channel tagged its third straight quarter of positive organic growth, and the firm's Private Markets AUM grew 13% YoY to $85.2 billion, keeping it on track for its 2027 target.

2026 Outlook

But on the earnings outlook, I’m disappointed with recent trends. EPS growth remains positive, but consider that FY 2026 per-unit earnings are just $3.47; that figure was $4.02 at the time of my previous assessment. A bottom-line growth rate in the mid-single digits appears to be the norm (we were looking at low teens in late 2024).

Indeed, the sell side has turned less constructive on AB, as evidenced by a high 7 downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to zero upgrades.

AB: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $3.60 or normalized EPS and apply a 12x P/E, units should trade near $43. I reduced my multiple assumption by one figure, resulting in a $3 lower intrinsic value target.

AB: Mixed Valuation Metrics

Key risks for AB include persistent market volatility and macroeconomic uncertainty, which continue to drive risk-averse behavior and elevated outflows in its active equity strategies. The firm's financial performance is closely tied to the health of global equity and fixed-income markets, so an inflation-led bear market could hit AB AUM particularly hard. Recall that March AUM fell 4.7%, and we could see that trend persist. Further AUM dips could lead to lower dividends in the future.

There are also operational risks, including the potential for record-high institutional pipelines (which are not legally binding) to fail to fund as expected. Finally, the business remains sensitive to shifting inflationary pressures, geopolitical instability, and potential changes in the regulatory or tax treatment of publicly traded partnerships.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q2 2026 earnings date of Thursday, July 23 BMO. Units trade ex a $0.83 dividend on Friday, May 8.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares now only mildly below my fair value target and soft net flows, AB’s technical situation is weaker than it was in 2024 and early 2025. Notice in the chart below that shares went from holding in a solid uptrend to now trading sideways. Furthermore, the long-term 200-day moving average is now on the decline, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. Price is also below the 200-DMA, which is not a good sign.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has ranged in a neutral zone lately, confirming the choppy price trend. There’s now a high amount of volume by price both below and above the current spot, which augurs for more sideways trading in the months to come. For now, resistance is between $43 and $44 (near my intrinsic value objective), while support is in the high $30s.

AB: Uptrend Morphs Into Chop, Falling 200-DMA

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on AB. Units are modestly below my fundamental target, while the chart is weaker heading into the middle of 2026. But the yield is high, and AB’s management team raised the dividend earlier this year.

data.title: Heartland Express: Macroeconomic Risks, Overvaluation, And Overbuying May Block Further Upside
data.description:
With tariff wars and skyrocketing oil prices, inflation appears to be gaining momentum once again. In the midst of all this, freight and logistics businesses like Heartland Express, Inc. (HTLD) are facing further risks and potential weakness in their business operations. I may have been a bit too early to downgrade its rating to hold, but the current market dynamics say it is a logical decision. Fundamentals are still robust but do not justify further upside, supported by its valuation and technical caution.

Challenges Remained Intense, But It Was Still Fighting

Freight and logistics have been vulnerable to market volatility in the past year. In many industry players I’ve covered, I’m sure I’ve already explained how softer consumer spending continued to affect their performance. In Heartland Express, Inc., you may have also seen how it continued to plunge amid weaker demand and freight rate volatility. This is amplified by stubborn inflation despite the efforts of the Fed to keep it low. With that, many businesses like HTLD had to balance or match their operating capacity with the prevalent market dynamics. This was evident in its most recent performance.

In Q1 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $176.3M, down by -19.7% YoY from $219.4M. With this double-digit revenue drop, HTLD does not indicate a strong recovery anytime soon. We can attribute this to soft consumer spending in line with the rising prices. This was supported by its capacity contraction. With lower demand through loads/volumes/shipments, it also had to lower its fleet capacity to save more money and avoid unnecessary expenses.

However, this appeared to be its most decent performance in four quarters. In fact, its YoY revenue drop was much better than in my previous coverage at -26.1%. In Q2 2025 and Q3 2025, the revenue change was -23.4% and -24.5%, respectively. Somehow, this was a slight recovery or improvement from its rock bottom in 2025. Moreover, its capacity contraction worked to its advantage. In fact, its total operating expenses dropped by -23.4%, which completely offset the revenue drop. This also showed improved efficiency despite the increasing cost pressures.

The only problem was that the revenue drop was still so massive that HTLD could not cover all operating expenses. In the end, the operating margin remained negative, although it has already improved to -1.9% versus -6.7% YoY.

Risk Factors

As a player within the freight and logistics industry, HTLD remains highly vulnerable to inflationary headwinds. Last month, it regained its momentum to 3.3%, which was its 20-month high. This was also the first time that inflation touched 3.0% again in six months. Because it is still above the 2.0% target, one can say that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. This is not surprising since it also reveals how the external disturbances and headwinds can easily affect domestic prices. Aside from tariff wars, the geopolitical tension in the Middle East is a primary contributor to it. Currently, oil prices are still skyrocketing at $98.11 per barrel. Even during the ceasefire period, WTI did not go below $80 per barrel. With that, we can expect its direct impact on fuel prices.

HTLD may impose higher fuel surcharges. However, the domino effect of higher oil and fuel prices may amplify inflation and squeeze the purchasing power of consumers and the production capacity of suppliers. In addition, HTLD may experience higher cost pressures. So, its revenues and margins may remain squeezed. It may also have to raise wages so employees can keep up with inflation. This is a crucial aspect because driver shortages are still rampant in the US.

Risk Mitigants

Despite these headwinds, I believe that HTLD has some strengths and opportunities to protect its operations. However, I also believe this is also limited to risk mitigants and not growth drivers in the short run.

One aspect to consider is the unexpected benefits of the current market situation. HTLD’s negative but improving margins amid its capacity contraction show its resilience and adaptability to market volatility. If the situation escalates, there is a possibility for more smaller players to shut down or reduce their delivery capacity further. With their limited capacity network and smaller size, they may be less resilient to inflation and market volatility than HTLD. Note that there have already been multiple bankruptcy filings in the past year. Considering this, it may become a survival of the fittest situation in the short run. The more resilient and larger players like HTLD may capture the demand of smaller players who will temporarily/permanently exit the market. Supply may shrink or contract faster than demand, allowing HTLD and peers to maintain their pricing flexibility. Of course, this is still a supposition since we haven’t accurately measured the actual extent of the Middle East tension and its impact on oil and fuel prices.

Meanwhile, its robust liquidity assures investors of its sustainability and dividend longevity. Its cash and cash equivalents more than doubled to $44.5M from my previous coverage. This is not a surprise because of its improving margins. Aside from that, we can add back and deduct the non-cash items to/from its net loss. These include depreciation and amortization and gain on disposal of PP&E. With that, I am almost certain that HTLD generated positive operating cash inflows to cover capex and dividends. Aside from that, HTLD maintains prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. As you can see, maturity remains long and decent because no amount will mature within the year. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of only 1.0x, which means that HTLD only needs a year to repay the whole amount. Indeed, its leverage remains low, proving it has enough buffer to sustain its capacity.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its price ratios. But just like in my previous coverage, both ratios show that HTLD has already increased more than necessary. For instance, P/B shows it trades at 1.37x its book value versus the four-year average of only 1.21x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $11.66. This justifies its rebound from the 2025 lows, but the increase/buying appears overdone. Likewise, P/S shows that HTLD trades at a higher sales multiple and suggests a TP of $10.17. Additionally, this shows that HTLD is already trading at its highest level since 2022.

Technicals

HTLD’s technicals are bullish as the price rebound continues with higher highs and lows. In addition, it trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is stronger than in the past 50 and 200 days. However, you should note the recent flattening of the 50-day SMA, which suggests emerging caution among investors, which can be in line with its stretched valuation.

Its MACD and RSI also show the bullish trend as they remain consistent with the price trend. The MACD line is still above the signal line within positive territory along with the histogram. This implies that buying volume is still increasing and outpacing selling volume. However, RSI shows that HTLD is already overbought, which may prompt profit-taking and a dip. This supports the flattening 50-day SMA as the stock becomes more expensive.

Conclusion

Heartland Express, Inc. continues to experience the unfavorable impact of macroeconomic headwinds, but its strategic capacity management shows its resilience and prudence. This is supported by its robust Balance Sheet to survive the challenges. However, current growth prospects are limited as inflation may squeeze demand and margins. Valuation and technicals adhere to it as overpricing and overbuying show downside risks. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: HP Inc.: Cheap On Paper, Challenged In Reality
data.description:
I buy into quality companies when they're on a massive sale and hold them for the long term. Buying into fear is a double boost to total returns. We're profiting from a high dividend yield that can be reinvested into other bargains, and over the long term, our portfolio is benefiting from multiple expansion. If done right, we're outperforming the market, achieving staggering total returns, with companies that others would deem "boring" at best.

After doing this kind of investing for many years, the best way to describe it is compound interest on steroids with below-average risks. It's a lonely road, but a profitable one.

I always start my analysis by screening for companies that are trading at low P/FCF ratios. Preferably in the single digits, but I'm willing to pay up if the company has low debt levels or is of great quality. Out of the thousands of companies that my screener spits out, I may only invest in a handful. AI isn't going to replace due diligence anytime soon when it comes to value investing with a focus on quality.

In my view, quality does not necessarily mean high growth, but consistency and predictability. When it comes to free cash flow generation, we don't want negative surprises. This way, we have a higher chance of avoiding costly value traps.

HPQ is Cheap on Any Valuation Metric

HP Inc. (HPQ) is a company that has been showing up on my screeners lately. Trading at a 6.24x P/FCF ratio and a 7.46x P/E, HP is exceptionally cheap. Despite this, the company's profitability over the last decade has been stable. EPS and dividends grew because of substantial share buybacks. It's a shareholder-friendly company that distributes most of its FCF to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

In my view, it's a classic Benjamin Graham bet. If you hold a diversified portfolio of companies with the same risk/reward profile as HP at current valuations, you're going to do well over the long term. Some of them will turn out to be value traps, but the winners will more than make up for the losses.

For my own concentrated portfolio, however, HPQ is a pass even at the current low valuations. I'd revisit my thesis if shares were to decline even further from here. The lower the valuation, the lower the risks and the higher the expected total return. Since I don't hold HPQ myself, I won't issue a "Buy" rating and will rate the shares a "Hold."

The reason behind my cautiousness, despite the stable cash flows over the last decade, is simple. The company's high-margin printer business is in a slow secular decline. The PC business is growing over the cycles, but it's a cyclical and commoditized low-margin business. HP is mostly competing on price in this segment. There is some brand equity, as the brand is widely known, but most customers wouldn't pay a premium for an HP computer if the competition were to offer a better deal.

With its limited pricing power, not only does lower demand disproportionately hurt profitability, but higher costs also eat into margins. The prices for memory have spiked. As a business with low margins, that's bad news, and it's already showing in sharply lower profitability in the PC segment this fiscal year.

Selling PCs is highly cyclical. Demand tends to depend on the economy and on refresh cycles. Even the market leader, Lenovo, has low gross and net margins, erratic cash flows, and a stock price that has gone nowhere for decades.

It's a tough business, and as a long-term investor, I'm not a fan of holding companies with below-average profitability unless they're screaming value. HPQ isn't quite there yet.

HP: A Story of Printers and Computers

If I had to describe HP's business model in one sentence, I'd say it sells printers and computers. The printing segment is the high-margin cash cow, but it's declining year after year.

If it weren't for the secular decline, the printing segment would be a business I'd love. You give away the devices for a low price and charge high margins on the ink and toner cartridges. It's like the highly profitable razor and blade model.

The issue is that printing is in a secular decline, and HP faces competition from low-cost third-party providers selling ink and toner cartridges. Offices and people simply print out less than they used to, and it shows in the numbers.

Net revenues in the printing segment declined from $18 billion to $16.7 billion for a CAGR of -3.75% from 2023 to 2025. Margins held up well, but I'd be cautious about expecting them to hold up forever. Printer unit volumes have been down for years. If you're selling substantially fewer printers, that obviously will hurt demand for cartridges, and it will weigh on margins eventually.

Year: 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021
Printer Unit Volume: -4.2% -7.2% -10.2% -12% +3.0%

As a long-term investor, I also look at long-term trends. With a cyclical company like HP, we're gaining a better understanding of how the business is performing over different cycles.

Since its full year as a standalone company in 2016, HPQ's operating profit in the printing segment has been flat. It had $3.1 billion in operating profit in 2016 and $3.1 billion in 2025. Margins improved from 17.1% to 18.7%.

If unit volumes keep declining — and there's no reason to believe that this long-term trend will reverse — at one point, margins will decline as well. So, the printing segment is endangered. The profits from the PC segment will have to make up for it, but it's not a quality business by any means.

The Computer Segment

The computer segment, or the Personal Systems (PS) segment as HP calls it, has shown growing revenues but declining margins over the last three years.

Earnings from operations were essentially flat from 2023 to 2025. Over the last decade, operating profits in the segment almost doubled from $1.15 billion in 2016 to $2.05 billion in 2025 for a CAGR of 6.63%.

The unit volumes look more promising than the printing segment, at least in recent years, but overall, even the computer unit sales aren't in high-growth mode by any means.

Year: 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021
