PS Unit Volume: +4.3% +3.3% -14.5% -16.9% +9.8%

And we shouldn't forget, the printing segment remains the core driver of profitability, and it's caught in a serious secular decline. The computer segment is a cyclical and low-margin business. Overall, HP's net margin of 4.86% in 2025 is low. So, altogether, I'd describe HPQ as a low-margin cash cow with long-term challenges.

A margin collapse in the printing segment could quickly turn HPQ into a value trap because the main drivers of total returns are the dividends and share buybacks. It's the main risk for shareholders, despite its rock-bottom valuation. A rapid margin collapse is almost never priced in, not even at a 7x P/E.

Free Cash Flow and Shareholder Yield

I might not be enthusiastic about the overall quality of HP's underlying business, but any serious analysis should always include the valuation. At 7x earnings and a 6% dividend yield, the shares are cheap. At least some of the risks have been priced in.

Despite the flattish operating profits over the last decade, EPS and FCF/share are up sharply since 2016, mostly because of generous buybacks.

Earnings have been in a free fall since 2023, but that's likely just the cyclical nature of the industry and demand normalization after COVID. Lenovo, the market leader, also saw a sharp downturn in profitability during the same period.

As long as HP can generate healthy amounts of free cash flow to fund the dividends and buybacks, the shares are offering value at current levels. So, a "Sell" rating would be overly harsh, but the quality of the underlying business also doesn't justify a "Buy" rating at current valuations. A neutral "Hold" rating seems appropriate given the risk/reward profile from here.

Conclusion

HP isn't an exceptionally high-quality business but a low-margin cash cow with some long-term challenges. Given its risk profile, the current valuation isn't as convincing as it might appear at first glance.

For now, I'll let this one pass. I'll keep it on my watchlist until the valuation reflects a screaming bargain that fully compensates for the risks involved in the secularly declining printing business.

It's a lonely road, but skipping the riskier deals is how we reduce risks and enhance returns. That said, HPQ could have a place in a well-diversified portfolio if you're less risk-averse than I am. And in my experience, many investors out there are.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: Solid Growth But At A Full Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide (HLT) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining about 50%. The company has benefited from ongoing strength in travel spending among higher-income consumers, which has offset some pressure at the lower end. Its significant organic growth pipeline and fee revenue streams have also helped boost growth. That said, its Q1 results were solid but not perfect, sending shares a bit lower in early trading. I last covered Hilton in February, rating the stock a “Hold” as strong fundamentals offset its full valuation in my view. Since then, shares are basically flat, consistent with the rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit HLT.

Q1 results were solid

In the company’s first quarter, Hilton earned $2.01 per share, which beat estimates by $0.04. While revenue was up 9% to $2.9 billion, this was slightly below consensus, given some softening in the Middle East. Revenue per available room (RevPAR) is one of the most important metrics in the hotel sector, speaking to the demand and efficiency of its operation; it grew a very solid 3.6% in the quarter. Hilton also continues to return capital to shareholders and boosted its outlook.

Hilton generated $901 million of EBITDA in Q1. This was up 13% from last year thanks to ongoing margin expansion as it builds scale. Franchise fees jumped $71 million, or 11%, to $696 million, reflecting the continued growth in its system. Incentive fees grew a relatively modest 5% to $76 million, likely reflecting some signs of softening in travel demand, which I will discuss further below. Hotels it owns grew revenue 6% to $249 million while their expenses fell by $4 million to $235 million, contributing to much of the margin expansion.

Pipeline remains very impressive

During the quarter, Hilton added 11,000 net rooms, and its system is 6.3% larger than a year ago. It also added another 26,000 rooms to its pipeline, which has reached 527,000. Even as it continues to bring new hotels online, its backlog has grown 5% from last year. These rooms under development provide a clear backlog of future revenue growth. With ~1.35 million rooms currently in service, this pipeline represents ~37% cumulative growth. As such, we should see HLT sustain 6+% annual room growth at least through 2030.

Systemwide, occupancy was up 1.4% to 67.4%, led by strength in Asia and Europe. Unsurprisingly, the Middle East fell 4%. Undoubtedly, the outbreak of the Iran war during the quarter hurt travel in the region. I expect this decline to worsen in Q2, as there was just 1 month of impact in Q1. Even with a ceasefire, I expect international business travel to the region to run significantly lower for some time. Importantly, this region is only 4% of the business, so any decline has a modest overall impact.

While Hilton is largely a U.S. business today, I would reiterate that its pipeline is tilted more overseas. Here, the Middle East is 10%, or ~2.5x its current business weight. Asia is nearly 4x as large at 34%. Over time, this will make Hilton a more globally diverse business. These markets are likely to face more pressure from geopolitical uncertainty and higher oil prices in 2026, though with this pipeline being multiple years in nature, I do not believe this adds meaningful risk to the pipeline.

Growth was balanced across brands

As noted above, the system grew RevPAR by 3.6%. In Q4, we saw a clear delineation with higher-end hotels outperforming its more value-oriented brands. Growth was more evenly distributed in Q1. Tru, its lowest-cost brand, still increased RevPAR by 2.6%, while the Waldorf Astoria grew just 0.8%. Mid-tier brands, like Curio, Canopy, and Tapestry, led the way, each growing over 6%. This balanced growth was actually encouraging for me to see. The “K-shaped” economy is an ongoing concern, especially in discretionary categories like travel, but that fear did not materialize in Q1.

Still, I am cautious on travel spending among lower- and middle-income households this year. We are still in the infancy of this energy price shock, especially as most trips are booked weeks or months in advance. We are likely to see fuel costs lead to higher airfare and squeeze household budgets to an extent, which may lead consumers to pull back on some discretionary activity. Especially with financial markets so robust, higher-income consumers are likely to be insulated from this shock, but that may not be true further down the income spectrum. As such, I expect this divergence between brands to reappear, especially during the summer vacation season.

The balance sheet supports capital returns

Hilton maintains a reasonable balance sheet with $12.5 billion of debt; importantly, it has no material maturities within the next year. That leaves leverage at just about 3.1x, a healthy place to run given its recurring cash flows. With its cash flow, Hilton continues to aggressively return capital to investors. It pays just a token 0.2% dividend, and instead, it focuses on buybacks. In Q1, it repurchased $860 million of stock. Thanks to these buybacks, its share count is down about 4.5% from a year ago. Hilton is on track to meet my ~$3.3 billion buyback expectation this year.

Alongside results, Hilton increased its EPS guidance range by $0.14 to $8.79-$8.91 while continuing to project 2%-3% RevPAR growth and net unit growth of 6%-7%. When looking at guidance, I reiterate the fact that HLT’s outlook does not include the benefit of future share buybacks, which is a reason it tends to run structurally below consensus and increases during the year (consensus is currently $9.05). The benefit of ongoing buybacks should be worth at least $0.22, bridging the gap between HLT’s view and consensus.

With a full valuation, shares are just a "Hold"

Back in February, I was targeting $9.10-$9.30 in EPS. While Q1 was in line with my view, I do see incremental risk for the rest of the year from the energy market dislocations, especially on overseas travel. As such, I am now looking for $9.05-$9.20 in EPS. Given its reliance on franchise fees, Hilton is less exposed to a modest softening in travel demand, and I do not view current macro volatility as changing the long-term tailwind from its pipeline.

Similar to when I last covered Hilton, it scores very poorly on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener, getting an “F” rating. Shares trade at a substantial premium to the consumer discretionary sector, though its forward P/E trades at only a modest premium to its own history. In other words, much of its relative premium is structural in nature, reflecting its clear organic growth pipeline and franchise fees. I expect these tailwinds to continue to support its valuation. That said, given how elevated valuation already is and potential cyclical pressures on travel, I struggle to see the case for further multiple expansion from here.

Shares are trading at about 36x, which is by no means cheap. I continue to see the company having ~13% EPS growth through at least 2031, reflecting 6%-7% room growth, 3%-4% share count reduction, and modest operating leverage. With EPS doubling in about 5.5 years, Hilton can steadily grow into its multiple and generate market-like performance. At this multiple, though, I believe the outperformance has largely occurred, especially with a mixed near-term outlook for travel demand. As such, I remain a “Hold.” With a supportive buyback and backlog, there is no rush to sell, but I believe investors should wait for shares to pull back to $300 before buying.

data.title: Ventas: Senior Housing Growth Appears Priced In
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2026, Ventas (VTR) has managed to marginally outperform U.S. REIT peers held in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), building on the strong returns achieved in 2025.

While solid Q1 2026 results and an increased 2026 outlook have provided a near-term tailwind for the shares, my overall rating remains a Hold. My neutral investment outlook is justified by:

  • VTR trades at 21.9x its expected 2026 normalized FFO, reduced to 20x if we account for further occupancy gains and associated margin improvements.
  • VTR employs modest leverage, providing near-term safety against unexpected downturns, but likely resulting in a drag on long-term shareholder returns.
  • VTR's U.S. occupancy has already increased in 2024-2026, indicating that a key driver of normalized FFO growth may peter out in the next few years.

Q1 2026 Results Overview

VTR delivered normalized FFO of $0.94/share, up 9.3% Y/Y, benefiting from a larger portfolio as well as a 8.7% Y/Y increase in same-store cash net operating income [NOI].

NOI growth was again driven by gains in the senior housing operating portfolio [SHOP], where same-store segment NOI surged 15.4% Y/Y, with SHOP NOI now accounting for 58% of Ventas's overall Q1 2026 NOI. NOI growth for the remainder of the portfolio was much more modest, at around 2% Y/Y.

The outsized increase in SHOP NOI was partially driven by a 3.7% occupancy improvement in the U.S. SHOP portfolio, reaching 87% in Q1 2026. Even so, U.S. occupancy remains below the 97.2% level observed in Canada, indicating that Ventas could potentially see another few years of above-average NOI growth, assuming U.S. occupancy continues to grow.

I should note that SHOP growth is not coming from occupancy gains alone. Revenue per occupied room [RevPOR] increased 5% Y/Y, reflecting strong pricing power.

To sum up, the only red flag I would highlight with regard to VTR's Q1 2026 results is that performance is increasingly being driven by developments in the SHOP segment, amplifying risks if operating conditions deteriorate. With this caveat in mind, let's discuss the REIT's marginally increased 2026 outlook.

Higher 2026 Outlook

Alongside its Q1 2026 results, VTR also increased its full-year normalized FFO outlook to $3.82-3.89/share, or $3.86/share at its midpoint, up 0.8% relative to previous expectations. The improved guidance reflects marginally increased occupancy expectations, as well as higher SHOP investment volumes of $3 billion, up $0.5 billion relative to initial expectations.

The company ended Q1 2026 with net debt of only $12.76 billion, indicating that net debt only accounts for 23% of the $55.5 billion enterprise value. As such, the change in market pricing for Fed rate cuts from 2026 to 2027 has had a limited impact on VTR's 2026 financial outlook.

All in all, we see that VTR is taking advantage of growth opportunities in the SHOP segment to increase investments, conservatively funding its growth with equity capital, as reflected in a 504 million expected share count for 2026, notably higher than the 486.7 million shares outstanding at the end of Q1 2026.

Valuation and Rating

After a strong start to 2026, VTR trades at 21.9x its 2026 normalized FFO, arguably a quite elevated level for a commercial REIT. The silver lining is that the normalized FFO is achieved with a modest leverage level. At the same time, strong SHOP performance should continue to drive above-average normalized FFO growth for at least a few more years.

Indeed with NOI growth running at 2% outside of the SHOP segment (i,e. in line with inflation and not a major differentiator relative to other REIT peers), the million dollar question with regard to VTR clearly remains for how long can the company sustain its SHOP momentum.

I think increasing occupancy remains a low hanging fruit for VTR. If we assume that occupancy will ultimately rise in the high 90s percent, we see that the company can reasonably deliver three more years of 3% annual occupancy gains (against the current level of 87% occupancy for the U.S. portfolio). The good news is that higher occupancy also increases margins (the cash NOI margin jumped 1.4% to 29.1% in Q1 2026).

I estimate that higher margins alone could boost normalized FFO by about 1%. U.S. occupancy gains of about 9% in total, increasing occupancy to the high 90s percent (roughly 3% annually spread across several years), could likewise lift normalized FFO by about 8%. As such, my calculations show that VTR's normalized FFO should grow by approximately 9% in excess of what other REITs can deliver, benefiting from strong momentum in the SHOP segment.

The normalized FFO growth can be even higher if VTR can sustain above-inflation RevPOR increases, although I see such increases as unsustainable in the long term. Likewise issuing shares at the elevated 21.9x normalized FFO multiple to fund growth opportunities could boost normalized FFO growth. As the company's size grows however, incremental investments have a diminishing impact on overall returns.

To sum up, I estimate that VTR can reasonably reach a normalized FFO of about $4.20/share (i.e. 9% higher than current levels) over the next few years even before factoring in price increases of about 2% annually. I think approaching VTR's valuation from this viewpoint allows investors to gauge the company's idiosyncratic growth profile, as opposed to growth coming from general economic factors such as 2% inflation which are common for all U.S. REITs (notwithstanding somewhat higher inflation in 2026 due to high energy prices).

Even after accounting for this outsized growth however, we see that the normalized FFO multiple remains quite elevated, at about 20.2x. As such, I think that the market has already priced in VTR's significant growth pipeline, confirming my previous Hold rating on the shares.

Risks

In light of increased NOI exposure to the SHOP segment, a deterioration in operating performance in senior housing arguably remains the principal risk in the VTR investment case. While the company is keen to point out tailwinds such as an ageing U.S. population and limited new supply hitting the market, it is also worth highlighting that recent RevPOR increases may make senior housing unaffordable. This in turn may result in VTR's occupancy stabilizing around current levels, even as the overall total addressable market (broadly defined as U.S. citizens aged 80 and over) continues to grow.

I would also highlight that VTR's leverage is quite low, at only 23% of enterprise value. While keeping a low debt level helps during unexpected downturns and adverse financing conditions, it also acts as a drag on returns during favorable periods. As such, I would argue that the company's low leverage is at odds with the robust operating performance in recent years. In essence, VTR seems to be preparing for a downturn that may never come, hurting long-term shareholder returns in the process.

Last but not least, as with any REIT that seeks to attract investors with its dividend distributions (VTR only resumed dividend growth in 2025 after keeping the dividend unchanged in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic), a sustained increase in long-term interest rates may reduce the relative attractiveness of VTR relative to other quasi fixed-income alternatives (I see REITs as closely resembling an inflation linked bond, with payments potentially growing with inflation and construction costs if a REIT can maintain pricing power).

Conclusion

With U.S. markets close to all-time highs, finding real value opportunities among large cap stocks is quite hard. While I do see a path to VTR outperforming the S&P 500 over the long term (largely driven by high current S&P 500 valuations), the path for VTR to outperform REIT peers is arguably quite narrow, especially after recent occupancy gains in 2024-2026.

I should note that being sceptical on VTR has not been a winning strategy, as the REIT's premium valuation has kept expanding even after my very first Hold rating in 2024. Back then U.S. occupancy stood at 81.7%, indicating that roughly 40% of the one-off occupancy gain is behind us (assuming VTR eventually reaches a U.S. occupancy rate on par with its Canadian portfolio, which is far from certain). Even so, the normalized FFO multiple has jumped from 19x in August 2024 to 21.9x in April 2026.

While I agree that the continued rise in occupancy and associated margin improvements will easily lead to VTR reaching a normalized FFO of $4.20/share (before inflation driven growth, which is arguably achievable for most REITs, hence not a real catalyst in my opinion), this is more than reflected in the current share price, hence warranting a Hold rating on VTR.

For investors who plan on holding their VTR shares even after the recent rally, I see high SHOP exposure, low leverage, and a potential rise in long-term rates as principal risks in the investment case.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Verizon Q1: Promising Customer Growth, Shares Attractive
data.description:
Verizon’s (VZ) new CEO, Dan Schulman, had his work cut out for him when he took the reigns of VZ in late 2025.

Coming into the role, the mobile carrier was losing core customer wireless customers, and it was falling further behind T-Mobile (TMUS) following its merger with Sprint.

Sure enough, in the first full quarter under Schulman’s leadership, VZ outperformed expectations. The company added net postpaid phone connections for the first time since 2023, and it also raised its full-year profit guidance.

Improving VZ’s customer growth and reigning in costs were two big intentions of Schulman stepping into the CEO role. It’s perhaps fortunate timing that this fell into place for him in Q1.

Granted, one can’t exactly say VZ’s Q1 story was solely attributable to Schulman’s leadership. After all, it was his first quarter in the pilot’s seat. VZ’s quarterly results, nonetheless, were promising, and I believe the prospects of a continued turnaround could be a key catalyst for VZ moving forward.

I am bullish on shares of VZ, and I believe long-term investors could be rewarded with both attractive share price upside, as well as steady recurring dividend income.

VZ Stock Key Metrics

Based on its most recent quarter, VZ is growing total revenues at about 3% YOY. And within this topline is mobility and broadband service revenue, which grew 1.6% in Q1. It’s worth noting that the 1.6% growth was despite an 80-basis-point hit from customer credits tied to the January network outage.

From a total customers standpoint, VZ ended Q1 with 146.8M total wireless retail connections. In addition, during Q1, VZ also closed on its +$9.6B acquisition of Frontier Communications. I expect this to further support the base of connections in the periods ahead.

I also believe the closure of the deal expands VZ’s fiber footprint and strengthens its ability to bundle wireless and home internet, a strategy VZ has repeatedly emphasized as central to improving customer lifetime value and reducing churn over time.

Despite this improving profile, VZ still trades at just 9.5x forward earnings and about 5.3x forward cash flow.

I view both as steep discounts to sector medians. With a nearly 6% dividend yield and Wall Street seeing roughly 10% upside, I view the valuation as attractive, especially in-line with VZ's improving fundamentals.

VZ Q1 Results Recap

As mentioned in my stock metrics section, topline revenues grew 2.9% YOY, while mobility and broadband service revenue increased 1.6% despite an 80-basis-point headwind from customer credits related to the January outage.

On the earnings call, VZ mentioned that Q1 should represent the low point for service revenue growth this year. This was based on trends showing improvements as the quarter progressed. This should bode well for the forward outlook, in my view.

While it may seem as if VZ’s Q1 was mixed based on the news ticker showing a beat on earnings and a miss on revenues, I view the quarter as a ‘beat’ overall.

My view that the quarter was a ‘beat’ stems from the subscriber momentum figure. Here, VZ posted 55K phone net adds, the first positive Q1 result since 2013. In addition, it was a 344K improvement versus last year.

Broadband was also strong with 341K net adds, including both fixed wireless and fiber. Just as important, churn improved throughout the quarter, with consumer postpaid phone churn exiting March below 0.85%. At the same time, acquisition and retention costs fell roughly 35%.

This in turn drove much healthier unit economics. Those better customer economics flowed directly into profitability, with adjusted EPS rising 7.6% YOY and adjusted EBITDA margins expanding 140 basis points.

What Is The Outlook For VZ Stock?

I view the outlook ahead as positive, and I attribute this in part to the raised forward guidance that was provided on the earnings call.

VZ now expects adjusted EPS growth of 5% to 6% for 2026, up from the prior 4% to 5% range. Additionally, postpaid phone net adds are projected to land in the upper half of the 750K to 1M range for the year.

Meanwhile, mobility and broadband service revenue growth guidance of 2% to 3% was reaffirmed, with Q1 described as the low point as trends improved throughout the quarter.

Free cash flow (“FCF”) is expected to grow roughly 7% or more this year, supported by lower acquisition costs, improving churn, and operating expense savings as part of the company’s +$5B cost reduction program. VZ also expects to end the year with more than 32M fiber passings. I view these as all positive marks for the forward outlook.

Is VZ Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I view VZ as a 'buy' at current trading levels.

In Q1, VZ grew customers, improved churn, lowered acquisition costs, expanded margins, raised guidance, and generated stronger cash flow all at the same time. That’s not something VZ investors have been able to say in years, especially with regards to customer growth.

These improvements are happening while the company is also reshaping its operating model with its new CEO, integrating Frontier to expand its fiber footprint, and leaning into a strategy that I believe should structurally improve customer lifetime value over time.

Despite the improvements, the stock still trades at less than 10x forward earnings and just over 5x forward cash flow, while paying a dividend yield near 6%.

Consensus targets see roughly 10% upside from here, and that’s before factoring in the potential for multiple expansion if VZ continues to turn around its operations.

As this turnaround further materializes, I believe that investors will continue to be paid attractively with a high yielding and well-covered dividend payout. All considered, for long-term investors I view shares as a ‘buy.’
